motivation: I chose software because of the satisfaction of watching a small solution make thousands of people's work easier. A simple membership-tracking tool I wrote for a student club in university ended up solving a real, recurring problem — that's what pulled me deeper into this field.
values: Transparency — sharing the reasoning behind decisions with the team
Continuous learning — every project has a lesson worth extracting
Sustainable pace — moving fast matters, but moving without burning out matters more
workStyle: I prefer clarifying ambiguity early and shipping small, frequent increments to get feedback fast. I enjoy thinking through a teammate's blocker with them almost as much as writing code myself.
careerGoals: Over the next 3-5 years I'm aiming for a role that shapes a larger engineering organization without losing technical depth — think engineering manager or senior staff engineer. I want to keep working on tools that improve developer experience.
strengths: Explaining complex technical topics clearly to stakeholders at different levels
Breaking ambiguous problems into small, testable steps
Staying calm and prioritizing well under pressure
growthAreas: Delegation — learning to hand things off earlier instead of doing them myself
Prioritizing written documentation before diving into code

selfAssessments.note: I enjoy clarifying goals and routing work to the right people, while occasionally throwing in an unconventional idea.
I prefer resolving disagreements around 'what does the data say' rather than 'who's right'.
I prefer giving teammates room to reach their own solutions and making decisions with the team wherever possible.

behavioralStories.situation: When I became tech lead for a 5-person team at Nova Software, the code review process varied wildly by person — some PRs sat for days while others were barely reviewed at all.
behavioralStories.task: I was responsible for getting the team to a consistent quality bar without slowing the review process down.
behavioralStories.action: I worked with the team to write a short code review checklist, wired automated lint/test checks into the PR pipeline to take formatting nitpicks off human reviewers' plates, and started a 15-minute weekly 'review calibration' session so everyone aligned on what the standard actually meant in practice.
behavioralStories.result: Within three months, average PR review time dropped by 35%, the number of bugs reaching production noticeably decreased, and the team reported the process now felt fair and predictable.

behavioralStories.situation: During the decision to move to a microservices architecture, a teammate felt further splitting the existing monolith was premature optimization and that the internal architecture should be simplified first instead.
behavioralStories.task: I needed to reach the technically right call while keeping trust intact within the team.
behavioralStories.action: I reframed the discussion away from 'who's right' and into a short decision doc listing which metrics and scenarios would favor each approach; both sides stated their assumptions explicitly, and we ran a one-week timeboxed proof of concept to test them.
behavioralStories.result: The experiment confirmed the migration was the right direction but should happen more gradually; my teammate's concern was folded into the migration plan as a phased roadmap, and the decision ended up being owned by the whole team rather than imposed.

aiIntroTopics: My leadership style and how I work
Challenges I've faced and how I handled them
